subject: Pulmonology
question: A 60-year-old man with heart failure presents with right-sided pleural effusion. Thoracentesis shows: Protein 2.1 g/dL (serum 6.5), LDH 120 U/L (serum 220 U/L). What type of effusion is this?

options.text: Transudative effusion due to heart failure
options.explanation: Light's criteria: Pleural protein/serum protein ratio = 2.1/6.5 = 0.32 (<0.5 = transudate). Pleural LDH/serum LDH = 120/220 = 0.55 (<0.6 = transudate). Transudates are caused by increased hydrostatic pressure (HF) or decreased oncotic pressure (cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome).
Exudate would have protein ratio >0.5 or LDH ratio >0.6. This effusion meets transudate criteria. Parapneumonic effusion is exudative with high LDH and low glucose.
Malignant effusion is exudative with high protein and LDH. Often bloody. This is transudate. Cytology would be needed to confirm malignancy.
Hemothorax requires pleural fluid hematocrit >50% of blood hematocrit. Not present here. This is a transudate.
Chylothorax has milky fluid with high triglycerides (>110 mg/dL). Not present here. Usually due to thoracic duct injury (trauma, malignancy).

educationalObjective: Apply Light's criteria to differentiate transudative from exudative pleural effusions. Transudate: protein ratio <0.5 and LDH ratio <0.6.
keyTakeaway: Light's criteria: Transudate = protein ratio <0.5 AND LDH ratio <0.6. Exudate = either ratio above threshold. Heart failure = most common transudate.
